coredata.dc:title: Rapid synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots by microwave method for sensitive detection of co(II) in water environment
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Junmei
Zhang, Yu
Miao, Xirui
Li, Haichao
Tao, Chunying
Liu, Jin
Yang, Xudong
coredata.dc:description: Novel nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were prepared by microwave method using o-phenylenediamine and formamide as raw materials, and the N-CDs were used successfully for sensing Co(II) on the basis of photoluminescence quenching. The structural characterization of the obtained N-CDs was thoroughly performed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectroscopy. The optical properties were also determined by absorption and fluorescence spectra. N-CDs have bright yellow fluorescence emission and high quantum yield (29 %), with a detection limit as low as 0.38 μM. In order to detect Co(II) more conveniently and quickly, N-CDs/PVA fluorescence composite film was prepared by compounding N-CDs with polyvinyl alcohol. The fluorescence composite film combines the fluorescence characteristics of N-CDs and the stability of polyvinyl alcohol. The application range of Co(II) visual detector in the water environment is expanded.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microwave synthesis
Carbon dots
Detection of co(II)
Fluorescent sensor
Fluorescence composite film

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of N-CDs/PVA fluorescence composite film
Applications in the natural water environment
Sensitivity and selectivity for the detection of Co(II)
Possible response mechanism of N-CDs towards Co(II)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of N-CDs. (b) Size distribution of N-CDs.
(a) XRD pattern. (b) FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs (inset: photographs of N-CDs under sunlight and UV light illumination). (b) Fluorescence spectra of the N-CDs. (c) CIE coordinates of the N-CDs. (d) Excitation-emission matrix.
(a) Fluorescence intensity response of N-CDs after adding different ions. (b) The fluorescence response histogram of N-CDs in the presence of interfering ions.

(a) The fluorescence spectra of N-CDs at different Co(II) concentrations (1-10,000 μM). (b) The fluorescence intensity ratio of N-CDs responds to Co(II). (c) Images under the sunlight. (d) Images under 365 nm UV lamp.
Photographs of fluorescent composite films after reaction with different concentrations of Co(II) (0-1000 μM) under fluorescent lamp and 365 nm UV lamp irradiation.
(a) The UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs in the absence and presence of Co(II). (b) The amplified UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs in the absence and presence of Co(II).
Schematic illustration of the synthesis route leading to the detection of Co(II) with N-CDs.
Determination of Co(II) in Nanhu Lake water and tap water.

head.ce:title: Rapid synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots by microwave method for sensitive detection of co(II) in water environment

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, Changchun 130012, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, Changchun 130012, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were prepared by microwave method using o-phenylenediamine and formamide as raw materials, and the N-CDs were used successfully for sensing Co(II) on the basis of photoluminescence quenching. The structural characterization of the obtained N-CDs was thoroughly performed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectroscopy. The optical properties were also determined by absorption and fluorescence spectra. N-CDs have bright yellow fluorescence emission and high quantum yield (29 %), with a detection limit as low as 0.38 μM. In order to detect Co(II) more conveniently and quickly, N-CDs/PVA fluorescence composite film was prepared by compounding N-CDs with polyvinyl alcohol. The fluorescence composite film combines the fluorescence characteristics of N-CDs and the stability of polyvinyl alcohol. The application range of Co(II) visual detector in the water environment is expanded.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Cobalt is one of the heavy metal elements in nature and is widely used in various fields, including batteries, catalysts, alloys and pigments. Co(II) is an essential trace element for the human body. It enters the human body through breathing and daily diet in daily life and plays an important role [1]. However, excessive intake of Co(II) can cause a series of health problems, such as asthma, rhinitis, decreased lung function, vasodilation and cardiomyopathy in humans or animals [2,3]. At present, WHO has stipulated that the concentration of Co(II) in drinking water should not exceed 40 μg L-1 [4]. At present, there are many methods to detect Co(II), mainly including UV–vis absorbance [5], chemiluminescence [6], flame atomic absorption spectrophotometry [7], electrochemical methods [8], etc. However, these methods not only require tedious pretreatment processes, but also require expensive and sophisticated equipment and high energy consumption. It is difficult to achieve real-time detection in the actual detection process. Therefore, it is necessary to design a highly selective and sensitive Co(II) detection method to prevent the adverse effects of Co(II).

As a new type of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials [9–11], carbon dots (CDs) have excellent optical properties [12], low toxicity [13], environmental friendliness [14] and good biocompatibility [15]. It has been used in optical sensing [16–18], environmental monitoring [19–21], biological imaging [22,23], medical diagnosis [24] and other fields. In particular, carbon dots have been used as fluorescent sensors to detect metal ions [25], anions [26] and biomolecules [27]. Pang prepared a pH-sensitive CDs by one-step hydrothermal method using m-phenylenediamine as raw material. The fluorescence spectrum of this CDs has a good linear relationship with the concentration of hydrogen ions, so it has the potential to be used as a probe for detecting pH [28]. Li et al. prepared CDs by one-step hydrothermal method with good detection effect on Hg(II), and cleverly used as anti-counterfeiting and information encryption of invisible ink [29]. However, the previously described CDs preparation method has the disadvantage of long reaction time, while the microwave method has the advantages of simple equipment, short reaction time and low cost. For example, Zhang et al. used citric acid and m-phenylenediamine as raw materials to prepare N-CDs and P-CDs by microwave method for the detection of ciprofloxacin, and realized the visual detection of ciprofloxacin in milk and Malatang for the first time [30]. Zhang et al. prepared blue fluorescent carbon dots B-CDs by microwave-assisted hydrothermal method, which could detect low concentration of Fe(III) and the quantum yield was as high as 87.07 % [31]. Li et al. prepared green fluorescent carbon dots N-CDs by microwave method. Fe(III) was added to quench the fluorescence, and tetracycline was added to enhance the fluorescence [32].

In this study, N-CDs with yellow fluorescence emission were rapidly synthesized in aqueous solution by microwave method, and the quantum yield reached 29 %. N-CDs have high sensitivity and selectivity to Co(II) in the water environment and with a detection limit of 0.38 μM. In order to facilitate portable and visual Co(II) detection, a portable Co(II) detection fluorescent film was developed by combining N-CDs with polyvinyl alcohol. The visual detection effect is remarkable and has a good potential for application in environmental detection.

O-phenylenediamine was purchased from Beijing Chemical Factory, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich (USA), and formamide was obtained from the Aladdin Industrial Company (China). All of these chemical agents were of analytical grade and were utilized without further purification. All experiments used ultrapure water obtained using a Milli-Q purification system (Merck, Germany). The solutions of metal ions were prepared from AgNO3, FeCl2, NaCl, FeCl3, CaCl2, K2Cr2O7, Ni(NO3)2, KCl, CoCl2, CrCl3, MgCl2 and HgCl2 (purchased from the Aladdin Industrial Company (China)).

Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope (TEM) (FEI, USA). Cary 5000 ultraviolet-visible-near infrared (UV–Vis- NIR) spectrometer (Agilent, USA). An LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA). SmartLab X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (Rigaku, Japan). A Nicolet IS50 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR) (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA).

Firstly, 0.5 g of O-phenylenediamine was dissolved in 35 ml of ultrapure water, and then 5 ml formamide was added. Ultrasound until completely dissolved. The solution was transferred to a 100 ml microwave reactor particular reaction container, and the reaction was carried out at 180 °C with the power of 400 W for 30 min (Scheme 1 ). This was followed by purification with a dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 1000 Da for 24 h to remove other impurities. After dialysis, N-CDs with yellow fluorescence emission were obtained and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-CDs/PVA fluorescence composite film
ce:section.ce:para: The 2.5 g PVA was dissolved in 45 ml ultrapure water and stirred evenly with a glass rod. The mixed solution was transferred to a three-necked round-bottomed flask and heated in an oil bath at 80 °C for 30 min. Then add 5 ml of N-CDs solution and stir for 10 min to be thoroughly mixed. The obtained mixed solution was transferred to a beaker and stood for 15 min to eliminate bubbles. The defoamed mixed solution was tiled in a clean mold, and the N-CDs / PVA fluorescence composite film was obtained after 36 h at room temperature.

In order to realize the specific detection of metal ions by N-CDs, standard solutions containing Ag(I), Ca(II), Co(II), Cr(III), Cr(VI), Fe(II), Fe(III), Hg(II), K(I), Mg(II), Na(I) and Ni(II) at a concentration of 0.1 M were selected for the detection experiments. In order to explore the sensitivity of N-CDs to the response of Co(II), different concentrations of Co(II) solution (0-10,000 μM) were prepared. The fluorescence spectra were obtained at the optimal excitation of 420 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications in the natural water environment
ce:section.ce:para: In order to test the practical applicability of the N-CDs sensor in sample analysis, the actual samples (tap water and lake water samples) were tested and analyzed. First, the water sample was pretreated, and the water sample was centrifuged at 10000 r/min for 10 min. The supernatant was taken and filtered using a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to remove the dissolved and particulate matter in the water, and the Co(II) in the real water sample was detected by the standard recovery method.

The morphology and size distribution of N-CDs were characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1(a), N-CDs have a microscopic appearance that is nearly spherical in shape and uniformly dispersed, as well as appearing monodisperse. Fig. 1(b) shows the particle size range of N-CDs synthesized by microwave method is 1–3 nm, and the average particle size is 1.8 nm.

The components and contents of N-CDs were examined using an EDS spectrum. Fig. S1 shows the results, with C, N, and O contents (wt%) of 81.08 %, 12.78 %, and 6.14 %. As shown in the XRD pattern of Fig. 2(a), there is a broad peak at 23°, which proves that N-CDs do not form a lattice structure of carbon atoms [33,34]. Fig. 2(b) shows the FT-IR data of the N-CDs. The characteristic absorption band at 3427 cm−1 is associated with the stretching vibration of OH or NH [35,36], while the broad peak at 3243 cm−1 can be ascribed to the stretching vibration of NH [37], indicating that abundant -NH2 groups were present on the surface. Stretching vibrations of CO at 1675 cm-1 [38,39], stretching vibrations of amide linkage(O=C-NH) at 1632 cm-1 [40,41], while stretching vibrations at 1595 cm−1 and 1500 cm−1 are caused by the stretching vibration of CC [42,43]. Further, the peaks at 1272 cm−1 and 1024 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of C-O-C [44] and CO [45]. Finally, the peak at 750 cm−1 was assigned to CH out-of-plane deformation vibrations on the substituted benzene ring of aromatic hydrocarbons [46,47].

To further confirm the above FT-IR results, the X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS) of the N-CDs-C was also investigated. In the XPS spectrum, the C, O, N, B and S elements can be observed in the four different CDs (Fig. S2). As shown in Fig. S2(a), there are three typical peaks at 531 eV (O1s), 399 eV (N1s) and 284 eV (C1s) in the XPS, suggesting that our prepared N-CDs are consisted of O, N and C elements. Fig. S2(b) C1s shows four different characteristic peaks at 284.8 eV (C − C) [48], 285.7 eV (C − N) [32], 288.1 eV (C=O) and 288.9 eV (C-C=O). As depicted in Fig. S2(c), the high-resolution spectrum of N1s could be fitted to peak positions at 399.4 eV(O=C-N) [29] and 398.35 eV (−NH2) [32]. As shown in Fig. S2(d), the O1s XPS spectrum manifested two peaks at 530.95 eV(CO) and 532.3 eV(C-C/C-OH) [49]consistent with the FT-IR distribution. The above findings demonstrate the successful synthesis of nanometer-sized N-CDs using the microwave method.

In order to obtain N-CDs under the optimal experimental conditions, we changed the experimental time or temperature while controlling other experimental conditions. The experimental results are shown in Fig. S3. When other experimental conditions remain unchanged, as the temperature increases, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs first increases and then decreases, reaching the highest at 180 °C. Therefore, the optimal experimental conditions for N-CDs are 180 °C. At the same time, the experimental reaction time was changed under the same conditions, and the fluorescence intensity was the largest when the experimental time was 30 min. Therefore, we reacted the raw materials at 180 °C for 30 min to obtain the optimal N-CDs.

In order to further explore the optical properties of N-CDs, the UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectrum were measured at a constant concentration. As seen in Fig. 3(a), the absorption peak of the N-CDs appeared at 230 nm, assigned to the π-π* transitions (C=C) [50]. Furthermore, the spectrum displayed a broad absorption peak at 289 nm, which should belong to π-π* transition of CC bonds (sp2 carbon) [51,52]. Further, It can be seen that the best excitation of N-CDs is 420 nm, and the best emission can be obtained at 565 nm. The illustration of Fig. 3(a) shows that it appears pale yellow under sunlight (left), and emits bright yellow fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet lamp (right). In Fig. 3(d), a single emission center is shown for N-CDs with the emission maxima at 565 nm in Fig. 3(b). As the emission wavelength changes, only the fluorescence intensity changes, while the strongest fluorescence band at 420 nm remains constant, indicating that N-CDs are not excitation-dependent. As shown in Fig. 3(c), their coordinates are in the yellow region (0.44,0.51). All of the above proved the application prospect of N-CDs as fluorescent sensors.

The degree of stability is a significant feature in the practical application of fluorescence sensors. As a result, further experiments are designed to investigate the fluorescence stability of N-CDs. Fig. S4 shows that the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs changes depending on the acid-base environment. When the pH was kept between 6 and 13, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs remained relatively constant, but when the pH fell below 6, the fluorescence was quickly quenched. Low intensity in acidic environments is due to protonation of the surface groups, such as -COOH and -OH, which renders the emissive sites of the surface groups inactive [53]. This demonstrates that N-CD fluorescence is stable throughout the pH range of 6–13. Fig. S5 shows that the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs in different salt solution concentrations has no noticeable change, implying that N-CDs are less affected by salt solution and have greater salt tolerance. As shown in Fig. S6, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs is basically unchanged with the increase of irradiation time at 365 nm UV lamp, so N-CDs have good anti-UV performance. In Fig. S7, it can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs did not change significantly after 30 days. In summary, N-CDs have good optical stability and can be used as fluorescent sensors for the detection of actual environmental water samples, which has important research value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity for the detection of Co(II)
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further explore the application of N-CDs in ion detection, the specificity and sensitivity of N-CDs for ion detection were tested. The standard solutions of metal ions of Ag(I), Al(III), Ca(II), Co(II), Cr(III), Cr(VI), Fe(II), Fe(III), Hg(II), K(I), Mg(II), Na(I) and Ni(II) were added to the same concentration of N-CDs solution, and a blank control group was established. The fluorescence spectra recorded under the optimal excitation at 420 nm are shown in Fig. 4(a). It can be clearly seen that the Co(II) standard solution can cause pronounced fluorescence quenching of the N-CDs solution. In contrast, the standard solution of other metal ions has no significant effect on the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs. Therefore, it can be concluded that N-CDs have an evident specific selectivity for Co(II). In order to prove whether N-CDs have a specific response to Co(II) even in the presence of other metal ions during the actual detection process, the same concentration of metal ions and Co(II) were added to the N-CDs solution. The fluorescence emission spectra of the corresponding N-CDs under the optimal excitation of 420 nm were recorded. The blank control group was added and the fluorescence spectra of the two groups were compared to obtain a columnar fluorescence image in the presence of interfering ions, as shown in Fig. 4(b). In the blank control group, the N-CDs solution had a higher sensitivity when only Co(II) was added. In the presence of other interfering ions, the fluorescence quenching effect of N-CDs on Co(II) was not significantly affected, which shows that N-CDs have a strong anti-interference ability in practical detection applications. In summary, N-CDs have specific selectivity and excellent anti-interference for the detection of Co(II), which provides a good basis for the use of N-CDs as fluorescent sensors in detection.

Based on the high selectivity of N-CDs to Co(II), the sensitivity of N-CDs to Co(II) detection was further explored. Different concentrations of Co(II) solution (0–10,000 μM) were added to the same concentration of N-CDs solution, and the corresponding fluorescence spectra at 420 nm excitation wavelength were observed. Fig. 5(a) shows a gradual decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs solution when the Co(II) concentration was increased from 0 μM to 10,000 μM until the fluorescence was utterly quenched. There is a good linear relationship between the concentration of Co(II) and the fluorescence intensity, as shown in Fig. 5(b). The linear regression equation between fluorescence intensity ratio and Co(II) concentration in the range of 0–80 μM was calculated as y = −0.0009× + 1.0008 (R2 = 0.9924), and the detection limit of Co(II) response was 0.38 μM, which further proved that N-CDs had high detection sensitivity to Co(II). Fig. 5(c) shows that with the addition of different concentrations of Co(II) to diluted N-CDs, the color of the N-CDs solution changed from yellow to dark yellow under natural light. At the same time, the fluorescence color of N-CDs solution with different concentrations of Co(II) under the irradiation of 365 nm ultraviolet lamp changed from bright yellow to dark sunk, as shown in Fig. 5(d). The results show that the fluorescence color change of N-CDs can be directly used for visual detection of Co(II). This detection method is not only simple to operate, but also expands the detection range. In order to verify the detection effect of N-CDs, we compared the detection performance of the prepared N-CDs for Co(II) with other materials. The results are shown in Table S1·N-CDs have higher emission wavelength, lower detection limit and detection performance.

In order to detect Co(II) more conveniently and intuitively, a fluorescent composite film was prepared by combining N-CDs with PVP, and it was combined with Co(II) ion solution. As shown in Fig. 6 , after the addition of Co(II) solution, the N-CDs/PVA fluorescence composite film gradually changed from yellow to dark brown under sunlight. Similarly, the fluorescence intensity was significantly weakened under ultraviolet light irradiation, and the color was gradually quenched from bright yellow fluorescence with the increase of Co(II) solution concentration. Therefore, the N-CDs/PVA fluorescence composite film has high visual detection sensitivity and has potential application prospects in Co(II) detection.

In order to verify whether the prepared N-CDs can successfully detect Co(II) in the water environment, the tap water of the school and the water samples of the Nanhu Lake around the school were used as samples, and the concentration of Co(II) was detected by standard addition recovery method [54]. Table 1 shows that the recovery rate of Co(II) in South Lake water of Changchun City is between 99.38 % and 101.50 %, and the recovery rate of Co(II) in tap water is between 98.77 % and 102.10 %. At the same time, in order to further verify the accuracy of Co(II) in actual water samples of N-CDs, the ion content in water samples was detected by hash visible spectrophotometer [55]. It can be seen from Table 1 that the results obtained are basically the same, indicating that the prepared N-CDs can be used for the detection of Co(II) in water environment with good accuracy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible response mechanism of N-CDs towards Co(II)
ce:section.ce:para: UV–vis absorption spectroscopy further explored the quenching mechanism of N-CDs by Co(II). The static quenching effect is generally caused by the reaction of the fluorescent substance with the quencher to form a stable non-fluorescence complex, which will significantly change the fluorescent substance's ultraviolet absorption peak [3]. The absorption peak of N-CDs in the presence of Co(II), shown in Fig. 7 , shifted from 289 nm to 284 nm, proving that Co(II) changed the electronic structure of N-CDs by forming a stable non-fluorescence complex between surface functional groups of N-CDs and Co(II), along with energy transfer from the N-CDs to the complex [56,57]. The formation of non-fluorescent complexes not only leads to changes in the absorption spectra of N-CDs, but also affects the Stern-Volmer quenching constant KSV. The Stern-Volmer quenching constant KSV was studied at 20 °C, 50 °C and 70 °C. The value of KSV was determined according to F0/F-1, where F0 was the fluorescence intensity of the blank control group and F was the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs at different temperatures. As shown in Fig. S8, KSV decreases with the increase of temperature, which is attributed to the decrease of KSV caused by hindering the formation of non-fluorescent complexes at high temperature [58]. Therefore, it is speculated that the fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-CDs induced by Co(II) may be static quenching. In order to further verify the quenching mechanism of N-CDs, we experimentally obtained the fluorescence lifetime diagram of N-CDs and N-CDs + Co(II) at room temperature (Fig. S9). The fluorescence lifetimes were 1.77 ns and 1.81 ns, respectively, and the fluorescence lifetime did not change significantly [59,60]. It is further confirmed that the addition of N-CDs to Co(II) is caused by static quenching.

In this work, N-CDs with strong yellow fluorescence emission were rapidly prepared by microwave method using o-phenylenediamine and formamide as raw materials for the detection of Co(II) in aquatic environment. This method is not only efficient and environmentally friendly, but also has low cost. The N-CDs obtained a strong emission peak centered at 565 nm at an excitation wavelength of 420 nm, and the quantum yield can reach 29 %. The presence of Co(II) can form a non-fluorescent substance with N-CDs and undergo static quenching, resulting in fluorescence quenching of N-CDs. The prepared N-CDs were used to detect Co(II) with high sensitivity, and the detection limit can be as low as 0.38 μM. The prepared N-CDs/PVA fluorescence composite film has a good effect on visual detection of Co(II), which expands the application range of visual detector of Co(II) in the water environment.

Junmei Liu: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Yu Zhang: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Xirui Miao: Visualization, Investigation. Haichao Li: Supervision, Resources. Chunying Tao: Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Jin Liu: Visualization. Xudong Yang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
This research did not involve human or animal samples.

This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52273122), the Program of Jilin Provincial Department of Science & Technology (Grant No. YDZJ202401327ZYTS) and the Open Project of State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, Jilin University (sklssm2024034).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no competing interests.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2024.111830.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Pharmacology, A hazard evaluation of the reproductive/developmental toxicity of cobalt in medical devices

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.D. Monnot, M. Kovochich, S.B. Bandara, J.T. Wilsey, W.V. Christian, G. Eichenbaum, L.E. Perkins, P. Hasgall, M. Taneja, K.J.R.T. Connor, Pharmacology, A hazard evaluation of the reproductive/developmental toxicity of cobalt in medical devices. 123 (2021) 104932.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots: advances in nanocarbon applications
